Mr. Robert Stern 
Fair Political Practices Commission 
P.O. Box 807 
Sacramento, California 95804 
Dear Bob: 
May 22, 1978 
When I saw you on February 21, I gave you a copy of a letter I had written some 
time ago explaining the difficulty of making the numerous reports if an organiza-
tion supports a number of candidates in a number of different elections. 
The attached material is further proof of that difficulty. 
"Report #1
11 is the First Pre-election Report for the June 6 primary and is 
self-explanatory. The Distribution Sheet shows all the agencies we had to cover. 
The notes to Sacramento County, Simi Valley, and Los Angeles City were required 
because we had previously reported a contribution for elections within their 
jurisdictions. 
"Report #2"is the Pre-election Report for the Long Beach Special Runoff of May 9 
that had a closing date of April 24, one day following the closing date for 
IIReport #1." 
We made no contributions during that one-day period but had to make 
the report and send it to the entire Distribution Sheet as indicated. 
The multiplicity of reports is incurred only by those companies, unions, or 
organizations that support a number of candidates in a number of different 
elections. With primaries on different dates and some of those primaries having 
runoffs, it is extremely difficult to keep track of all the closing dates. 
We 
are required to send copies to every jurisdiction that received a previous report 
so that there are no gaps of reported time. 
The Federal Election Commission recognized a similar problem on a national level 
with the different primary dates of the several states. The FEe now perm; 
ticandidate Committees to report monthly from January through September and 
then use the regular pre-election and post-election reporting schedule for the 
general. 


Mr. Robert Stern 
May 22~ 1978 
Page Two 
Is it possible that the Fair Political Practices Commission could adopt some 
schedule that might be more realistic? 
Incidentally, since I have mentioned this matter previously to Assemblyman Paul 
Priolo, I have sent him a similar letter wi 
attachments. 
Sincerely, 
:jc 
Attachments

sections.question_synthetic: Union Oil Company seeks guidance on simplifying the burden of filing multiple campaign disclosure reports across different jurisdictions when supporting numerous candidates in elections with different closing dates and primary schedules.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document is primarily incoming request/correspondence, not an FPPC advice letter response. Contains formal letter from H.P. Shawlee (Union Oil) to Robert Stern (FPPC Counsel) dated May 22, 1978, plus attached Form 461 campaign disclosure statements. No QUESTION or CONCLUSION sections typical of FPPC advice letters. References letter previously sent to FPPC on February 21, 1978, and mentions similar communication to Assemblyman Paul Priolo. The document illustrates the procedural problem being raised but does not contain FPPC's response/advice.

Box 7600, Los Angeles, California 9005 Telephone (213) 486-6446 unl n H. P. Shawlee Manager, Civic Affairs Mr. Robert Stern Fair Political Practices Commission P.O. Box 807 Sacramento, California 95804 Dear Bob: May 22, 1978 When I saw you on February 21, I gave you a copy of a letter I had written some time ago explaining the difficulty of making the numerous reports if an organiza- tion supports a number of candidates in a number of different elections. The attached material is further proof of that difficulty. "Report #1 11 is the First Pre-election Report for the June 6 primary and is self-explanatory. The Distribution Sheet shows all the agencies we had to cover. The notes to Sacramento County, Simi Valley, and Los Angeles City were required because we had previously reported a contribution for elections within their jurisdictions. "Report #2"is the Pre-election Report for the Long Beach Special Runoff of May 9 that had a closing date of April 24, one day following the closing date for IIReport #1." We made no contributions during that one-day period but had to make the report and send it to the entire Distribution Sheet as indicated. The multiplicity of reports is incurred only by those companies, unions, or organizations that support a number of candidates in a number of different elections. With primaries on different dates and some of those primaries having runoffs, it is extremely difficult to keep track of all the closing dates. We are required to send copies to every jurisdiction that received a previous report so that there are no gaps of reported time. The Federal Election Commission recognized a similar problem on a national level with the different primary dates of the several states. The FEe now perm; ticandidate Committees to report monthly from January through September and then use the regular pre-election
embedding.summary: This document consists of incoming correspondence from Union Oil Company of California requesting the FPPC consider adopting a more realistic reporting schedule to address the difficulty of filing multiple disclosure reports (Form 461) across numerous jurisdictions when supporting candidates in multiple elections with staggered primary and runoff dates. The company includes sample reports and distribution sheets as evidence of the reporting burden.
